Problem Statement¶

ReneWind is a company focused on enhancing the efficiency and reliability of wind energy production. As wind energy systems involve complex machinery such as turbines, gearboxes, blades, and generators, unexpected failures in these components can lead to significant operational downtime and high maintenance costs.

To address this challenge, ReneWind aims to implement a predictive system capable of analyzing sensor-generated data from wind turbines to anticipate potential failures. By identifying which components are likely to fail, organizations can act proactively, performing repairs before catastrophic breakdowns occur. This approach not only minimizes downtime but also significantly lowers maintenance and replacement costs.

Objective¶

The main goal of this project is to leverage machine learning techniques to predict generator failures in wind turbines using sensor data. Specific objectives include:

  1. Developing predictive models that classify whether a generator is likely to fail (“1”) or remain operational (“0”).

  2. Tuning and evaluating multiple classification algorithms to identify the model with the highest accuracy and cost-efficiency.

  3. Reducing operational costs by prioritizing repair over replacement and minimizing unnecessary inspections through accurate prediction of failures.

  4. Providing actionable insights for maintenance teams to schedule timely interventions and prevent costly breakdowns.

Data Description¶

The dataset provided consists of transformed sensor readings from wind turbines, with confidentiality preserved by anonymization. The details are as follows:

  • Training set: 20,000 observations with 40 predictor variables and 1 target variable.

  • Test set: 5,000 observations with the same structure as the training set.

  • Predictors: Include sensor measurements related to environmental conditions (e.g., temperature, humidity, wind speed) and mechanical components of turbines (e.g., gearbox, blades, tower).

  • Target variable: Indicates generator failure, where 1 represents a failure and 0 represents normal operation.

  • Cost considerations:

    • Repairing a failure (True Positive): Lower cost than full replacement.

    • Replacing a missed failure (False Negative): Highest cost due to unexpected breakdown.

    • Inspection of non-failing units (False Positive): Lower cost than repair.

The dataset provides a foundation for building and evaluating predictive models that balance both accuracy and operational cost efficiency in wind turbine maintenance.

In [1]:
# Import libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix, roc_auc_score
from sklearn.utils.class_weight import compute_class_weight

import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Dense, Dropout
from tensorflow.keras.optimizers import SGD, Adam

memory usage: 6.3 MB

Observations

  • Dataset contains 20,000 rows and 41 columns (40 features + 1 target)

  • All features (V1–V40) are numerical (float type)

  • Target variable is binary (int type: 0 = No Failure, 1 = Failure)

  • Only V1 and V2 have missing values (18 each)

  • Remaining 38 features have no missing values

  • Dataset is clean and well-structured

  • Memory usage is efficient (~6.3 MB)

max 15.493002 13.089269 17.090919 13.236381 8.133797 6.975847 8.006091 11.679495 8.137580 8.108472 ... 23.633187 16.692486 14.358213 15.291065 19.329576 7.467006 15.289923 7.759877 10.654265 1.000000

8 rows × 41 columns

Observations

  • Most features have mean close to 0, indicating data is likely standardized/normalized

  • Features contain both positive and negative values, showing symmetric distribution

  • Standard deviation varies across features, indicating different levels of variability

  • Presence of extreme min and max values shows possible outliers in data

  • Difference between mean and median (50%) in some features shows light skewness

  • Features like V32, V36 show high spread (high std) indicates more variability

  • Target variable mean = 0.0555 indicates only ~5.5% failures

train['Target'].value_counts(normalize=True)
Out[11]:
proportion
Target
0 0.9445
1 0.0555

Observations

  • Class 0 (No Failure) = 94.45%

  • Class 1 (Failure) = 5.55%

  • Dataset is highly imbalanced

In [12]:
#Univariate Analysis
#1. Target Variable
sns.countplot(x='Target', data=train)
plt.show()

Dataset is highly imbalanced because count of 0 is very high in comparison to 1

In [40]:
#2. Feature Distribution
train.iloc[:, :10].hist(figsize=(12,8))
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations:

  • Most features are centered around 0

  • Distributions look approximately normal (bell-shaped)

  • Some features show wide spread means high variance

  • Few features show extreme values means possible outliers

In [14]:
#3. Boxplot (Outliers Detection)
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
sns.boxplot(data=train.iloc[:, :10])
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.show()

Observations:

  • Many features contain outliers

  • Outliers are expected in sensor data

  • No need to remove aggressively

In [15]:
#Bivariate Analysis
#1. Feature vs Target (Boxplot)
plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))  # one single figure

plt.subplot(1,3,1)
sns.boxplot(x='Target', y='V1', data=train)

plt.subplot(1,3,2)
sns.boxplot(x='Target', y='V2', data=train)

plt.subplot(1,3,3)
sns.boxplot(x='Target', y='V3', data=train)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations:

V1 shows a positive relationship with failure, V3 shows a clear negative relationship, while V2 has overlapping distributions indicating weaker predictive power. Overall, V1 and V3 are strong predictors, and the presence of outliers is consistent with sensor data.

In [16]:
#2. Correlation Analysis
plt.figure(figsize=(25,20))
sns.heatmap(train.corr(), annot=True, cmap='coolwarm')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observations

  • Most features show low to moderate correlation with each other

  • No strong multicollinearity means good for modeling

  • Few feature pairs show high correlation (positive/negative) that points to possible redundancy

  • Correlation with Target is generally weak, indicating:

    • No single feature strongly predicts failure

    • Model needs to learn combined patterns

In [17]:
#Data Preprocessing
#Total Missing Values() in training dataset)
print('Train dataset Total Missing Values:', train.isnull().sum().sum())

#Total Missing Values() in testing dataset
print('Test Dataset Total Missing values:',test.isnull().sum().sum())

Train dataset Total Missing Values: 36
Test Dataset Total Missing values: 11

Both datasets contain missing values

In [18]:
# If missing values exist
train.fillna(train.mean(), inplace=True)
test.fillna(test.mean(), inplace=True)
In [19]:
#Checking missing values again for both the dataset
print(train.isnull().sum().sum())

print(test.isnull().sum().sum())

0
0

After handling missing values, no missing values remain in the dataset.

In [20]:
# Split Data (No Data Leakage)
X = train.drop('Target', axis=1)
y = train['Target']

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, stratify=y, random_state=42
)

The data is divided into training and validation sets in an 80:20 ratio using stratified sampling to maintain class distribution.

In [21]:
# Handling Test Data
X_test=test.drop('Target', axis=1)
Y_test=test['Target']

Now testing data has same number of features as training data

In [22]:
# Scaling
scaler = StandardScaler()

X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_val = scaler.transform(X_val)
test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

Metric Selection

Chosen Metric: Recall + F1-score + ROC-AUC

Reason is as follows:

  1. False Negatives (FN) are very costly refers to missed failures leading to expensive replacement.

  2. So, we prioritize Recall (catch failures)

  3. Use F1-score for balance

  4. ROC-AUC for overall performance

In [23]:
#Base Neural Network (SGD)
#Model 1

model_1 = Sequential([
    Dense(64, activation='relu', input_shape=(X_train.shape[1],)),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

model_1.compile(
    optimizer=SGD(learning_rate=0.01),
    loss='binary_crossentropy',
    metrics=['Recall']
)

history_1 = model_1.fit(
    X_train, y_train,
    validation_data=(X_val, y_val),
    epochs=20,
    batch_size=32,
    verbose=1
)

Epoch 1/20

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/keras/src/layers/core/dense.py:106: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(activity_regularizer=activity_regularizer, **kwargs)

Epoch 20/20
500/500 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 1s 2ms/step - Recall: 0.8198 - loss: 0.0523 - val_Recall: 0.8514 - val_loss: 0.0536

Model 1 (SGD)

  • Moderate performance with lowest recall (0.8288)

  • Misses more failure cases

  • Not suitable due to high FN
In [24]:
y_pred = (model_1.predict(X_val) > 0.5).astype(int)

print(classification_report(y_val, y_pred))
print("ROC-AUC:", roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred))

125/125 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 1ms/step
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.99      1.00      1.00      3778
           1       0.99      0.85      0.92       222

    accuracy                           0.99      4000
   macro avg       0.99      0.93      0.96      4000
weighted avg       0.99      0.99      0.99      4000

ROC-AUC: 0.9254109853633412

Observations

  • Accuracy = 99% means Overall predictions are highly correct

  • Class 0 (No Failure):

    • Precision = 0.99, Recall = 1.00 gives almost perfect prediction

  • Class 1 (Failure):

    • Precision = 0.97 means most predicted failures are correct

    • Recall = 0.83 means ~17% failures are missed

  • F1-score (Failure) = 0.89 gives good balance between precision and recall

  • ROC-AUC = 0.91 gives strong model performance

In [25]:
#Model Improvements
#Model 2: More Layers added

model_2 = Sequential([
    Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

model_2.compile(optimizer=SGD(0.01), loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['Recall'])
model_2.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val, y_val), epochs=20)

Model 2 (Deep Network)

  • Improved performance with better F1-score and recall

  • Learns more complex patterns

  • Better than Model 1
In [26]:
# Model 3: Adam Optimizer
model_3 = Sequential([
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

model_3.compile(optimizer=Adam(0.001), loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['Recall'])
model_3.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val, y_val), epochs=20)

Model 3 (Adam Optimizer)

  • Faster convergence and improved recall (0.8964)

  • Balanced performance

  • More stable than SGD
In [27]:
#Model 4: Dropout
model_4 = Sequential([
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.3),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.3),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

model_4.compile(optimizer=Adam(0.001), loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['Recall'])
model_4.fit(X_train, y_train, validation_data=(X_val, y_val), epochs=20)

Model 4 (Dropout)

  • Highest accuracy (0.9940) and strong F1-score

  • Reduced overfitting

  • Very strong model
In [28]:
#Model 5: Class Weights
weights = compute_class_weight(
    class_weight='balanced',
    classes=np.unique(y_train),
    y=y_train
)

class_weights = {0: weights[0], 1: weights[1]}

model_5 = Sequential([
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

model_5.compile(optimizer=Adam(0.001), loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['Recall'])

model_5.fit(
    X_train, y_train,
    validation_data=(X_val, y_val),
    epochs=20,
    class_weight=class_weights
)

Epoch 1/20
500/500 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 3s 3ms/step - Recall: 0.8705 - loss: 0.3460 - val_Recall: 0.9054 - val_loss: 0.1942

Model 5 (Class Weights)

  • Highest recall (0.9189) detects maximum failures

  • But very low F1-score (0.6710) gives poor balance

  • Over-predicts failures
In [29]:
#Model 6: Deep + Dropout + Adam + Class Weights

model_6 = Sequential([
    Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.4),
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.3),
    Dense(32, activation='relu'),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')
])

model_6.compile(
    optimizer=Adam(0.0005),
    loss='binary_crossentropy',
    metrics=['Recall']
)

model_6.fit(
    X_train, y_train,
    validation_data=(X_val, y_val),
    epochs=25,
    class_weight=class_weights
)

Model 6 (Final Model)

  • High recall (0.9144)

  • Strong F1-score (0.9206)

  • Highest ROC-AUC (0.9551)

    • Best overall balance
In [30]:
#Model 7 SGD + Momentum (Improved SGD)
model_7 = Sequential()

model_7.add(Dense(64, activation='relu', input_dim=X_train.shape[1]))
model_7.add(Dense(32, activation='relu'))
model_7.add(Dense(1, activation='sigmoid'))

# SGD with Momentum
optimizer = SGD(learning_rate=0.01, momentum=0.9)

model_7.compile(optimizer=optimizer, loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

model_7.fit(X_train, y_train, epochs=20, batch_size=32)

Model 7 (SGD + Momentum)

  • Model achieved ~99.3% training accuracy.

  • Loss reduced to ~0.034, indicating good learning,

  • Model successfully converged with stable performance,

In [31]:
#Evaluate All Models on Validation Set
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score

def evaluate_model(model, X_val, y_val):
    y_pred = (model.predict(X_val) > 0.5).astype(int)

    acc = accuracy_score(y_val, y_pred)
    recall = recall_score(y_val, y_pred)
    f1 = f1_score(y_val, y_pred)
    roc = roc_auc_score(y_val, y_pred)

    return acc, recall, f1, roc

Getting accuracy_score, recall_score, f1_score and ruc_auc_score of all models on the validation set

In [32]:
#Compare All Models
results = []

models = {
    "Model_1_SGD": model_1,
    "Model_2_Deep": model_2,
    "Model_3_Adam": model_3,
    "Model_4_Dropout": model_4,
    "Model_5_ClassWeight": model_5,
    "Model_6_Final": model_6,
    "Model_7_SGD+Momentum":model_7

}

for name, model in models.items():
    acc, recall, f1, roc = evaluate_model(model, X_val, y_val)
    results.append([name, acc, recall, f1, roc])

results_df = pd.DataFrame(results, columns=["Model", "Accuracy", "Recall", "F1", "ROC-AUC"])
results_df

Model Accuracy Recall F1 ROC-AUC
0 Model_1_SGD 0.99125 0.851351 0.915254 0.925411

List of performance of models for comparison is given above.

In [33]:
#Sort by Recall
results_df = results_df.sort_values(by="Recall", ascending=False)
results_df
Out[33]:
Model Accuracy Recall F1 ROC-AUC
4 Model_5_ClassWeight 0.98125 0.918919 0.844720 0.951916
5 Model_6_Final 0.99000 0.914414 0.910314 0.954428
2 Model_3_Adam 0.99200 0.909910 0.926606 0.953367
3 Model_4_Dropout 0.99375 0.905405 0.941452 0.952173
6 Model_7_SGD+Momentum 0.99150 0.900901 0.921659 0.948862
1 Model_2_Deep 0.99175 0.887387 0.922717 0.942635
0 Model_1_SGD 0.99125 0.851351 0.915254 0.925411

Observations

  • All models achieve very high accuracy (>0.98), indicating that accuracy alone is not a reliable metric due to class imbalance.

  • Recall varies across models and is critical here, with Model_5_ClassWeight and Model_6_Final achieving the highest recall (0.9189), meaning fewer costly false negatives.

  • Model_4_Dropout achieves the highest F1-score (0.9461), showing the best balance between precision and recall, but slightly lower recall than the top models.

  • Model_6_Final provides the best overall performance with the highest ROC-AUC (0.9574) and strong F1-score while maintaining top recall.

  • Model_7_SGD+Momentum also performs consistently well across all metrics, indicating stable optimization improvements over standard SGD.

  • Models using advanced techniques (Dropout, Adam, Class Weights, combinations) clearly outperform the basic SGD and deep models, showing the importance of optimization and regularization.

In [34]:
#Select Best Model
best_model = model_6  # assume model_6 performed best

Reason For the Selection of Best Model:

  • The primary objective is to minimize False Negatives, as they lead to high replacement costs.

  • Both Model_5_ClassWeight and Model_6_Final achieve the highest recall, ensuring fewer missed failure cases.

  • However, Model_6_Final outperforms in terms of F1-score and ROC-AUC, indicating better overall performance and class separation. Therefore, Model_6_Final is selected as the best model.

In [35]:
#Final Prediction on Test Data
test_pred = (best_model.predict(test_scaled) > 0.5).astype(int)

157/157 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 2ms/step

Final Model predicted on test data

In [36]:
#Save Submission File
submission = pd.DataFrame({
    "ID": test.index,
    "Prediction": test_pred.flatten()
})

submission.to_csv("final_submission.csv", index=False)

Final Result is saved as final_submission.csv

In [37]:
#Final Evaluation Code

from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score, confusion_matrix

# Predict on test data
y_test_pred = (best_model.predict(test_scaled) > 0.5).astype(int)

# Metrics
acc = accuracy_score(Y_test, y_test_pred)
recall = recall_score(Y_test, y_test_pred)
f1 = f1_score(Y_test, y_test_pred)
roc = roc_auc_score(Y_test, y_test_pred)

print("Test Accuracy:", acc)
print("Test Recall:", recall)
print("Test F1-score:", f1)
print("Test ROC-AUC:", roc)

157/157 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 1ms/step
Test Accuracy: 0.9888
Test Recall: 0.875886524822695
Test F1-score: 0.8981818181818182
Test ROC-AUC: 0.9357177431235136

Observations:

  • High accuracy (0.9894) but not reliable due to imbalance
  • Recall (0.8723) is moderate implies some costly failures are missed
  • F1-score (0.9028) shows decent balance
  • ROC-AUC (0.9344) indicates good class separation
In [38]:
# Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(Y_test, y_test_pred)
print(cm)

[[4697   21]
 [  35  247]]

Observations

  • Very high TN (4701) indicates that model correctly identifies most normal cases.

  • FP (17) is low indicates few unnecessary inspections.

  • FN (36) is significantly reduced indicates fewer costly missed failures (good improvement).

  • TP (246) is higher that suggests better detection of actual failures.

  • Overall, improved recall and reduced critical errors, making the model more suitable for cost-sensitive scenarios.

Actionable Insights & Business Recommendations¶

Key Actionable Insights

  • False negatives are the most critical errors as they lead to high replacement costs.

  • Higher recall models are more suitable for this problem as they reduce missed failures.

  • The final model provides a good balance between recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

  • Accuracy alone is not reliable due to class imbalance.

  • Advanced techniques (dropout, class weights, optimizers) significantly improve performance.

Recommendations for Business

  • Deploy the final model to identify potential failures early.

  • Prioritize maintenance for predicted failure cases to avoid costly breakdowns.

  • Accept minor inspection costs (false positives) to reduce major losses.

  • Continuously monitor and retrain the model with new data.

  • Use model predictions to plan preventive maintenance and optimize operational efficiency.
